author: @Dodgerog
text: 3 bits of zip tie would do the same job with the rough part on the outside. The screw will easily cut a spiral into the plastic and push the ties firmly against the hole. It also helps to tap the plug /ties slightly into the hole by about 4mm as when the pressure is applied by the screw it's less likely to push the surface plaster out into a mound and weakening the fixing. 3d printing will also work 😅

author: @BeMidbar77
text: Good morning Rob, if you don't have wall plugs you, can always do it the old fashion way by using a splint of wood to push in the hole. This is a very strong solution that sometimes is even better than wall plugs.

author: @maranmarantakeiteazie
text: pure zen, rob.

author: @Nico-vh1qp
text: Bet it holds better too lol👍

author: @timothyneiswander3151

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: Flip it 90deg on it's side and it will be a lot stronger. Maybe annealing it in a toaster oven or dehydrator might bond the layers better.

author: @meditationsoundscapes5203

author: @stupidscruff
text: Or even cheaper, chop a piece of an old plastic pen and cut one end down to 4 quarters, insert and screw. The plastic at the cut end opens out to grip as you screw 😂

author: @edbrackeen5979
text:
If I may give you some lifelong vocational experience.
Don't use those.

Just simply cut a piece of a plastic zip tye and stick it in a hole very slightly larger than tye screw you are using and tuat way tye screw will be mostly in direct contact with the concrete. Holds much better and lasts a lifetime.

author: @timsmith1426
text: Thanks for posting, that's a great way to think about your tools! :)

author: @MichaelRada-INDUSTRY50

author: @VeniceInventors

author: @dj1NM3
text: Another thing which works well as a DIY wall-plug are wooden dowels. So if you're into woodworking, you could bash one of those into a hole in masonry/brickwork if you've run out.

author: @RJ-cx1gt
text: I used a piece of wood, was too large - so quickly made myself a forge in the shed and ‘knocked up’ a set of chisels, presto - a secure fixing for my shelf. (Respectful satire!, love your channel, been a fan for years).

author: @d.marbus1493
text:
You are a timelord. In the past, they used some wood (matchsticks and scaps) or (wet)paper filler with or without leftover plaster to fill holes or secure nails in walls.
I've got 💯 year walls and what you come across while renovating....

author: @joeschmo622
text: We used to just dip wooden toothpicks in elmer's glue, wedge them in, let dry, trim flush with the wall, then if needed just open up a pilot hole, and screw away.  The soft wood (pine?) and resiliency/flexibility of the glue would act the same, let the screw push radially outward and wedge up against the interior of the hole without the threads digging in.

author: @royksk
text: All you need is a bit of wood roughly shaped and banged in. I remember making twisted plugs with an axe. These were flat pieces of softwood shape a little like a propeller, about the width of a brick and the thickness of a brick joint. They were hammered into a cleaned out joint where a door frame was to be fitted. they protruded and were cut back to allow the door frame to fit snugly and the frame then screwed to the plugs. All covered by the architraves 🧐

author: @djnucker
text: You could have gone down the old fashioned road and simply hammered a piece of wood into the hole to screw into ?

author: @Mountaineer8523
text: Since my walls are solid, for a curtain Id just jam some toothpicks into the hole. if I'd get crazy and put up a curtain :D

author: @philip5940
text:
I've been known to glue a long fence paling/thinplank on wall above  architrave at top of window . When glue sets i can scew things on . For some reason i like using the silicone caulk tubes for the glue . It's bonding to a large surface area you see. If there's wallpaper , then maybe remove that portion first . Britain is a wallpaper nation but we ain't so much in Australia.

  A nice woodgrain paling gives a decorative effect.

author: @Chris-tea-far
text: Maslows law of instrumens says" if you have a hammer every problem is a nail" if you have a 3d printer you can make a raw plug.

author: @wikedawsom
text: I find myself saying more and more often "I bet I can print that"

author: @mark.guitar
text: We need to think a bit sideways sometimes. We have a tendency to file our thinking into "boxes" and have to be nudged to apply our brains (necessity is a mother...). I was faced with the same curtain pole conundrum 30 years ago on a Saturday evening, with no shops open 'til Monday. I just cut up a bit of scrap dowel and sawed the bits lengthwise about halfway through. The fixings lasted longer than the curtains did! Great little thought provoker mate.

author: @lagunafishing
text:
Not that anyone cares but I have to say this... my very first invention was a wall plug for hollow cavities in 1973 - before Rawl even existed!
I also designed and produced the worlds smallest wall plug at 3mm for modellers :)
Nice use of a 3D printer Rob!

replies.author: @Smo1k
replies.text: Rawlings made the Rawlplug company, making only rawlplugs, shortly after WW1, though...

replies.author: @lagunafishing
replies.text: @Smo1k They used jute as a plug for masonry instead of wooden dowels. Mine was designed specifically for hardboard stud cavity walls.

author: @robertsutherland5731
text:
Bought my 3D printer before moving to rural Bulgaria. If I need a hinge, print it. Broken parts are sorted (mostly) and I’ve even printed spare car parts that would’ve taken a month to get here.
I’m sure as the technology improves, there’ll be a time when the filaments become more and more robust…….and then it’ll really take off !

author: @lightcapmath2777
text: Out of curiosity, what would you say the cost to print it and time spent to make was??  DVD:)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a surprisingly interesting question mate - i went on Amazon and a strip of 96 6mm plugs cost £2.46 - or 2.56 pence per plug - i pay £15 per kilo for the plastic roll and used 1 gram - or 1.5 pence for the plug - even f you factor n the electric cost - it was cheaper to make it - astounding

author: @chrismalcomson2824
text:
I couldn't find the curtain rail fittings that I needed, so printed them. That was about 6 years ago. Since then, we sold the house and the new buyer is still using them.

I also printed shelf brackets for the bathroom and a cover for a microswitch that turns on the lights in the walk in wardrobe when you open the door.

3D printers are awesome! :)

text: Greetings from Ireland. When I'm stuck I generally pare down a piece of wood, works great.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

text: True :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @twestgard2
text: Okay but two or three toothpicks, or even a bit of twig, does the same thing with a lot less time, money, and energy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mate - i am not putting toothpicks or twigs in a newly decorated room - if I didn't have the printer I would have driven to the store for them and if they weren't in the store I would have ordered them from Amazon and waited

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Wood is a pretty common building material my friend. Holds screws great!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mate - building grade wood and old twigs found in the garden are not the same thing - what you are saying is fine if it happens to be a toilet roll holder in an old outside toilet

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I’m honestly a little surprised at your uncharacteristically conventional approach. Toothpicks are kiln dried pine, same as building lumber, and a twig stuck into drywall or plaster will quickly be dried and preserved to the same extent. I use such things all the time for screw holes that are too big.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: a twig is mostly sapwood mate - it has no strength when dry - toothpicks are just splinters you have crammed together - it will work as long as it is not weight bearing - but it is a poor job - I think this is enough talk about wall plugs - let's agree to disagree

author: @trevorgoddard2278
text:
That is what a 3D printer is for, making the things you need and only the things you need.
It is just a matter of getting out of the pre 3D printing way of thinking.
Once apon a time a Rawl plug was just a piece of wood hammered into the wall to make it possible to fix a nail or screw into brickwork, then someone thought if you use a plastic thing that expands in the hole when used it saves time and effort, now all we need is someone to redesign it specifically to be 3D printed, as the process has different strengths and weaknesses compared to injection molding.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i agree mate

replies.text: I still have a box of wall plug dowels my dad gave me when I bought my first house, hollow fluted hardwood with an 1/8" hole down the centre tapered at one end and square cut at the other. Not much use for modern thermal block interior walls but still my go to choice for brick or stone.

author: @NeoShameMan
text: Slowly going to the personal factory revolution mindset 😊 by the ways, replace the extruder woth a camera and a bunch of tools and your 3d printers become a perpetual gardener, you won't need a fridge those irish potatoes will grow themselves 😊 that's my goal anyway, to eschew a personal industrial revolution that meets the home appliance convenience. If possible, being able to build it from scratch and from scrape. The major roadblock so far is how possible it is to create a mechanical brain that can satisfy the basic algorithmique computation needed to run the personal autonomous factory 😮

author: @SeaforgedArtifacts
text: Hello Robert, In case you haven't heard, Lahaina Hawaii is completely destroyed with people lacking food, water, sanitation power and communications. I am part of a local CERT team who will be delivering and installing small independent solar power systems for those still there as well as radios. If you could recommend a list of your videos for me to download and take to the survivors for inspiration please let us know. I should be leaving within the week. 🙏❤️

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text: As you are the one that has received training in emergency response, then you will be in a better position than Rob to choose which videos will the most applicable for that type of disaster. And with the organisational skill you have been taught, you have a week to organise someone to search through the videos and index them, play them back,  etc.

replies.author: @SeaforgedArtifacts
replies.text:
@Nuts-Bolts How am I supposed to do that? 
I am busy actually moving the materials and do not have the extensive knowledge of Rob's video archive to come up with a list at this time. I already have a few videos but a list of video numbers would be helpful to get the YouTube downloader started.

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text: @SeaforgedArtifacts Disaster preparedness means having things already prepared and ready to roll, so if I could answer that there wouldn’t be any need for CERT. We could all just wait until things happened.

replies.author: @SeaforgedArtifacts
replies.text: @Nuts-Bolts dude, the whole damn city burned to the ground, ten thousand plus people lost all their homes and preps! but you know what hon, I hope you get to experience this situation from the other side someday... Nay, I figure you likely will

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text: @SeaforgedArtifacts If you have a high work load already, stay focused on that. Thinking of tangental extras on the fly - like videos -  may end with you getting there with mental and emotional fatigue already. (removed strickthrough format)

author: @davemillan3360
text: If you drilled diagonally inside the hole with a smaller drill so that the back end of the hole is larger than the front end, filled the hole with epoxy thickened with microfibers and let it cure, then drill a hole that is smaller than the screw in the set epoxy, it would probably hold as well. Maybe useful for those that don't have a printer yet.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tip mate - thanks for sharing

author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text: Haha, yes! I have so many fixtures with 3d printed parts!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice!

author: @mickdavis8521
text: If I was in that situation, not owning a 3D printer, I may have used a small dowel or even an appropriate twig.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That's awesome mate - but I am not putting old twigs in a newly decorated room - if I didn't have the printer I would have driven to the store for them - but it is a good tip - cheers

author: @banjax66
text:
So 20 minutes eatch way. pluss maybe 20 minutes in the hardwarte store.. = 60 minutes. 1 hour...
How long did it take you to design and print the rawl plugs?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: about 2 mins and 10 mins to print - i made a tea while i waited - i got it from thingiverse i showed the link in the video mate

author: @CCoburn3
text: It should hold a shelf as well as a commercial version. As long as you don't put too much weight on the shelf, it should be OK.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah i would think so too mate - but tbh I am arse covering n case someone does put up a shelf overloads it and blames me - you know what kind of world we live in!

replies.author: @CCoburn3
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yeah, I know how it is. And like I told a guy the other day, nothing is foolproof, because fools are so ingenious.

author: @davidresar8256
text: It seems like anytime I go looking for those items, I'm not always successful every 1st time. Spending more time getting less done. But a 3d printer could change so much! Great job, and I appreciate you sharing with everyone!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @fuiehfjfcnsl
text: Ein Kommentar

author: @peterkent2138
text: 3D printer is a very useful tool, got me out of trouble many times. I have made brackets, spacers, hooks and recently a rubber (TPU) washer for the shower head. all made to measure and a perfect fit. 10 mins on cad, have a coffee and the unobtainable appears before your eyes.

replies.author: @geoffkeller5337
replies.text: Predecessor to the replicators from Star Trek.

replies.author: @mightystudiosvids
replies.text: Agreed! having a little TPU on hand has been great for odd gasket and washer needs that come up around the house.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's science magic mate

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Being a smoker, I would 'ave thought Rob would 'ave just jammed match-sticks in 'ole.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they really don't lolol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: stopped smoking mate - no match sticks around

author: @alwardslab8732
text: You should try those pet bottle to 3d printer wire recycling setup, you know, in case of zombie apocalypse you could still 3d print too...

author: @spehropefhany
text: Giving you a 3D printer and CAD program is like giving Michelangelo a paintbrush and canvas.

replies.author: @geoffkeller5337
replies.text: Indeed.

replies.author: @unicornadrian1358
replies.text: I would have said Da Vinci for the fusion of art and science.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - cheers mate!

replies.author: @zylascope
replies.text: Good one!

replies.author: @replikvltyoutube3727
replies.text: Wait till 4D printer drops

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome!
